skill.name: git-push
skill.description: Guides an AI coding assistant through pushing a local project to GitHub with checks for git setup, GitHub CLI login, large files, sensitive files, repository creation, commits, tags, and releases.
skill.summary: Push local projects to GitHub with guided safety checks, commits, tags, and releases.
skill.author: yunshu0909

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command examples and several high-severity patterns in SKILL.md. Review found no prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or executable malware; the high sensitive-file hits are safety checks for excluding secrets, and weak-crypto hits appear to be false positives. The skill still has medium operational risk because it instructs agents to run git and gh commands that can create repositories, modify Git metadata, publish releases, and force-push after confirmation.

medium_findings.title: External Git and GitHub CLI Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally directs the assistant to run git and gh commands for authentication checks, repository creation, commits, pushes, tags, and releases. This is appropriate for the skill purpose, but it can publish local project contents to GitHub and should require clear user consent before each publishing action.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and central to the skill workflow. The intent is legitimate DevOps automation, but the commands affect remote GitHub state and local Git history.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Git Operations Available After Confirmation
medium_findings.description: The workflow includes deleting local Git history during a restart path and using git push --force when remote history is incompatible. The skill requires warning and confirmation, but these operations can permanently overwrite local or remote repository history if misused.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive behaviors are described directly in the skill text. Built-in confirmation lowers likelihood, but the impact remains meaningful.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive File Discovery in Public Repository Workflow
medium_findings.description: For public repositories, the skill tells the assistant to scan for .env files, secret names, token names, key files, AI memory files, and private-looking content. This appears intended to prevent accidental publication, not steal secrets, but it means the assistant may inspect sensitive filenames or paths during use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive-file references are explicit and are framed as exclusion checks. No network exfiltration target or instruction to reveal secret contents was found.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns at several lines, but review found repository descriptions, examples, and command text rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithm use in the skill instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural-language metadata, file-size examples, or GitHub CLI examples. There is no cryptographic implementation at those locations.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Finding Is Attributable to Markdown Content
low_findings.description: The high-entropy alert points to SKILL.md as a whole. Review found a plain Markdown instruction file with Chinese text and command examples, not binary, encrypted, or obfuscated payload content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is readable Markdown from beginning to end. High entropy is plausibly caused by multilingual text and dense command examples rather than hidden code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Publishing Commands With User-Controlled Parameters
dangerous_patterns.description: Command templates include placeholders for repository names, descriptions, versions, release notes, URLs, and file paths. Agents using this skill should pass arguments safely and confirm resolved values before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command templates are visible and include user-supplied placeholders. The file is instructions rather than executable code, so exploitability depends on the calling agent implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Force Push Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The failure-handling table permits git push --force after warning the user. This can overwrite remote history and should remain an explicit, separately confirmed action.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The force-push instruction is direct and its destructive effect is well understood. The skill includes a warning, which reduces but does not remove risk.

content.value_statement: Publishing a local project can expose large files, secrets, or unfinished Git history. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through GitHub push workflows with checks, confirmations, and release steps.
actual_capabilities: Checks for git, GitHub CLI, GitHub login, and git user configuration.
Chooses between first push, daily update, restart, and release workflows.
Scans for large files before staging and warns about GitHub file limits.
Generates or updates .gitignore entries after user review.
Creates GitHub repositories, commits changes, pushes branches, tags versions, and creates releases.
Handles common push failures with guided recovery options.
limitations: Requires git and GitHub CLI to be installed and authenticated.
Can only publish to accounts available through the local GitHub CLI session.
Does not guarantee that every secret or private file will be detected.
Destructive actions such as force push still require careful human review.

use_cases.title: Publish a New Local Project
use_cases.description: Initialize Git, create a GitHub repository, set the remote, commit files, and push the main branch after safety checks.

use_cases.title: Ship Daily Repository Updates
use_cases.description: Review changed files, avoid newly added large files, create a concise commit, and push to the existing remote.

use_cases.title: Create a GitHub Release
use_cases.description: Suggest a version tag, summarize commits, create a release, and optionally upload release assets.

prompt_templates.title: First Push
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a new project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-push skill to publish this project to a private GitHub repository. Ask before creating the repository or pushing files.

prompt_templates.title: Daily Update
prompt_templates.scenario: Routine push for an existing repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-push skill to review my current changes, create a clear commit message, and push the update to the existing remote.

prompt_templates.title: Public Repository Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-push skill to prepare this project for a public GitHub repository. Check large files and likely sensitive files before any git add.

prompt_templates.title: Release Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for tags and releases.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-push skill to push pending changes, suggest the next version tag, generate release notes, and create a GitHub release after confirmation.

output_examples.input: Push this new project to GitHub as a private repository.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks local tools, scans for large files, confirms .gitignore changes, creates the repository, commits the project, and reports the GitHub URL.
The assistant reviews the changed files, flags any new large files, prepares a concise commit message, pushes to the configured remote, and summarizes the result.

output_examples.input: Create a release for this repository.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks the latest tags, suggests a next version, drafts release notes from commits, confirms details, and creates the GitHub release.

best_practices: Review the resolved repository name, visibility, file list, and remote URL before pushing.
Keep .gitignore updated before the first git add, especially for secrets and large files.
Use private repositories until you have completed a separate public-release review.
anti_patterns: Do not force push unless you are certain the remote history can be overwritten.
Do not publish a public repository before checking .env, key, token, and memory files.
Do not add large generated assets to Git when release assets or external storage are better choices.

faq.question: Does this skill push files automatically?
faq.answer: It defines a guided workflow. Repository creation, visibility, destructive actions, and pushes should be confirmed before execution.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: The local environment needs git and GitHub CLI. GitHub CLI must already be authenticated.

faq.question: Can it create public repositories?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow adds extra checks for likely sensitive files before publishing a public repository.

faq.question: Can it handle existing repositories?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports daily updates, repositories without remotes, and repositories that already have configured remotes.

faq.question: Does it create GitHub releases?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest a version tag, draft release notes, create the release, and upload optional assets.

faq.question: What is the main risk?
faq.answer: The main risk is publishing unwanted files or overwriting Git history if confirmations are ignored or misunderstood.
